back_translation: For this Melchizedek was king of the city of Salem, was priest of the Most High God, the one who met with Abraham when he returned from the slaying of the kings, and blessed him.
And Abraham gave a tithe from everything to him. First, the name 'Melchizedek' is translated 'king of righteousness,' and then also 'king of the city of Salem,' which means 'king of peace.'
The one who has no father, no mother, no genealogical line, no origin of days, no end of life, but was made to resemble the Son of God; he remains a priest forever.
Now consider how great this one was. Even Abraham the patriarch gave a tithe from the choicest of the spoils to him.

On one side, the descendants of Levi who received the priestly office have a divine command to collect a tithe from the people according to the law, that is, from their own brothers, although they too descended from the loin of Abraham.
But the one who did not trace genealogy from Levi received the tithe from Abraham, and blessed the one who received the divine promises.
And without any dispute, the one who is lesser inevitably receives a blessing from the one who is greater.
In one case, those who must die are the ones receiving the tithe; but in another case, there is testimony confirming the one who continues to live.
And it might be said, even Levi — the one who is on the receiving side of the tithe — also gave a tithe through Abraham.
For Levi was still in the loin of the ancestor when Melchizedek met with him.

Therefore now, if perfection could come through the priestly office of Levi — because the people received the law on the basis of this office — then why was it still necessary for another priest to arise according to the pattern of Melchizedek, and not according to the pattern of Aaron?
For when the priestly office is changed, there must inevitably be a change of the law as well.
For the one whom these words speak about belongs to another tribe, from which tribe no one has ever served at the altar.
For it is evident that our Lord descended from Judah, of which tribe Moses did not speak about priests at all.
And this point is even more clear when another priest arises according to the likeness of Melchizedek.
Who became a priest not according to a rule of a command concerning fleshly descent, but according to the power of a life that cannot be destroyed.
For there is testimony confirming: 'You are a priest forever in eternity, according to the pattern of Melchizedek.'
On one side, the former command was annulled, because of the weakness and uselessness of that command.
(For the law could not make anything perfect.) On the other side, there is the bringing-in of a better hope, by which hope we have drawn near to God.
And inasmuch as this matter did not happen without an oath — because on one side, those people became priests without an oath
but on the other side, Jesus became a priest with an oath, by the One who spoke to the Son: 'The Lord has sworn, and will not change His mind: "You are a priest forever in eternity."'
By that much, Jesus is therefore the guarantor of a better covenant.
Furthermore, on the side of those many priests, they became priests in great numbers, because death prevented them from continuing in office further.
But Jesus, because He continues forever in eternity, therefore has a priestly office that is permanent, that cannot be transferred to another.
Therefore He is able to save those who come to God through Him completely, because He always lives to intercede on behalf of those people.
For it was fitting for us to have such a high priest — namely, one who is holy, one who is innocent, one who is undefiled, one separated from sinners, and one exalted high above the heavens.

He did not have the need, like the other high priests, to offer sacrifices every day, first for the sins of his own self, and then for the sins of the people; because He did this once-for-all, when He offered Himself.
For the law appoints humans who have weakness as high priests, but the divine pronouncement of the oath which came after the law appoints the Son who has been made perfect forever in eternity.
